replies.author: @phrozac
replies.text:
The "white men bad trope" is hard to avoid when movies mostly cast white men in roles. I mean, unless it's something like Black Panther or Shaft, whenever a cast doesn't include a majority of white people in it, a certain demographic gets all fussy with cries of DEI. 

Also, women have been used as plot devices far more often than men have been. How often have you seen or read stories with a male protagonist who's wife or daughter was raped, murdered, or kidnapped vs the other way around? I find Simone's remake of Red Sonja's origin to be better than Thomas's and I've been an avid reader of Conan comics since the 80s.

I mean, think about it, Thomas's RS is raped, then granted incredible fighting prowess by a god as long as she doesn't have sex, but can only have sex by a man powerful enough to conquer her. How does that make any sense from a patron goddess? She's essentially a super hot virgin prize. She's designed as a boy's sex fantasy. Her potency centered on her vag. 

Conan's character isn't designed around his dick. He's capable and not reliant on god-given power or beholden to a vow of chastity. What Simone did was to make Red Sonja Conan's equal in all but physical strength. She gave RS agency and turned her from a cheap sex gimmick to an actually compelling character.

replies.author: @davidgantenbein9362
replies.text:
⁠@phrozacLol, if you would have read what I wrote above, you would know that I knew all of that already. In fact, you just omit some stuff to avoid saying „you are right“. Yes, Roy Thomas invented Red Sonja based on Red Sonya, but he invented her for Howard’s Hyborian Age, that’s why she is i.e. Hyrkanian, because that was the region Roy‘s story was taking place right then. The movie on the other hand is using these words without their proper context within the Hyborian Age.
Roy‘s Red Sonja was made to fit into Howard’s world, this movie simply doesn’t at all.
PS: Yes, the video mentioned that it’s mainly based on Dynamite‘s version, which doesn’t really remove the criticism, it just potentially moves some of it to Dynamite (I haven’t read all of their Red Sonja comics, so possible that they got it all wrong later on).

All I can tell is that their depiction of Hyrkanians is inconsistent since Hyrkanians are not fair-skinned blondes, but at the same time that doesn't mean a fair-skinned blonde cannot be Hyrkanian any less than a fair-skinned red-headed woman. replies.author: @phrozac
replies.text:
How can they destroy Rings of Power when there was no Rings of Power story to destroy? Rings of Power is based on a collection of fictional historical anecdotes told by unreliable narrators, not an actual narrative.

Wheel of Time was mostly intact except for their casting of the denizens of Emond's Field, which in the book is a largely homogenous population, not a delegation from the United Nations. The trimming of subplots and tertiary characters is a necessary trait for any adaptation of Wheel of Time that isn't 20 seasons long. We're lucky we got 3.

Willow was destroyed.

Witcher was mostly destroyed. They really should have focused more on Geralt doing monster of the week Witcher shit than jump straight into the political machinations so quickly. I lost interest pretty early into season 2, though season 1 wasn't that good either. Maybe things will turn around with the new guy and the show suffering from audience reception to the direction its taken in prior seasons? The Orville managed to shift tone after a poor first season and became a well-regarded series.

Red Sonja wasn't that bad. It's a fair bit better than the one from 1985 and about on par with 2011's Conan(which is an admittedly low bar). But as a character, Simone's version of RS is better than Thomas's, who was a virgin sex fantasy with god-given power, A little nudity would've been nice though. I mean, why else would they cast the actress from Revenge (2017)?

author: @j95lee
text:
In the first Conan movie the villain who led the village massacre chopped off Conan's mother's head while she was still holding his hand. Boom, Thulsa Doom is a terrifying bad guy and the character motivation is set in stone, all in the first 10 minutes. I didn't have to know anything about the source material to get involved. 

In this movie the guy who raided the village is an afterhought and the surviving heroine is a...... eco warrior? The antagonist is a soy boy who survived? Neither are primiarily motivated by vengeance? 

The movie industry is struggling now because it's being ran by people who don't understand the basics - writing, direction, fan expectation, the list could go on. They only heed their own agendas and address phantom fans who are apparently willing to spend loads of cash to see their visions fulfilled onscreen.

replies.author: @phrozac
replies.text:
The movie industry is not struggling. They understand the basics of writing, direction, and fan expectation as well as any previous generation if not better. What they're struggling with is distribution and our evolving methods of consuming the medium. If you look beyond the MCU and other summer tentpole style movies, you'll find a wide variety of genres bursting with creativity and originality. Take horror for example. Elevated Horror and sci-fi horror are having a bit of a renaissance. Directors big and small from Nolan and Villeneuve to Eggers, Aster, and Garland are raising the bar for genre films.

This Red Sonja isn't a great movie. It's not even a good movie. But it's better than the 1985 Red Sonja by a country mile. If you use 2025's RS as an example of Hollywood struggling, then the 80s must've been a dire time if their RS was any indicator. Every decade has stinkers, but they don't represent the industry as a whole. The 80s had plenty of garbage but also produced classics like Blade Runner, The Thing, Conan the Barbarian, Terminator, Aliens, and Predator.

replies.author: @j95lee
replies.text:
@phrozacmost horror movies is late stale. I wasn’t scared one bit by any of the conjuring movies.

They can’t tell a straightforward story. They can’t develop characters. The humor is always one note. The problem with the industry is lack of creativity, allegiance to the message, and not observing screen writing 101. You’re supposed to create an inciting incident and invite viewer attention in the first 15 minutes.

replies.text:
​@j95lee I'm not going off on tangents. I'm providing relevant examples of mainstream filmmakers as counterpoint, but it's okay if you don't understand. 

In short, you're being far too general with your central thesis, of which you've been moving the goalposts, having initially stated things as absolutes:

"The movie industry is struggling now because it's being ran by people who don't understand the basics - writing, direction, fan expectation, the list could go on. They only heed their own agendas..."

 Whereas now, you've retreated to subsets:

"*Mainstream* Hollywood can’t abide by movie making conventions and *often* focus on agenda and politics."

author: @momo.ru-chan
text:
One of the biggest personality shifts between comic Sonja (especially Gail Simone’s version) and the 2025 movie Sonja is how she expresses her values.

Comic Sonja (Simone’s run and others):

Carefree, not preachy: She doesn’t stop mid-battle to deliver speeches about justice. If she hates tyrants or slavers, she shows it by cutting their heads off, not by making moral proclamations.

Contradictory but human: She’ll help the weak, but she’ll also get drunk, insult kings, and fight for gold. Her morality isn’t framed as noble — it’s just her instinct and stubborn pride.

Actions > words: She rarely explains herself. People only know her beliefs because of what she does, not what she says.

Movie Sonja (2025):

Moralizing tone: The script often has her voice lofty ideals — freedom, justice, rising against tyranny. It makes her sound more like a revolutionary figurehead than a wandering mercenary.

Less gray, more black-and-white: She’s written as a clear “champion of the oppressed,” while comic Sonja often wandered in morally gray areas (taking jobs from shady people, helping only when it suited her).

Heroic archetype: The film reshapes her into a “chosen liberator” archetype, which makes her more generic and less roguish.

So while comic Sonja embodies her code through swagger and decisive action, movie Sonja feels almost like she’s lecturing — which undercuts the wild, unpredictable energy fans love in the comics.

The comics give us a flawed, larger-than-life swashbuckler, but the movie gives us a tragic, moralizing warrior-queen.
